title: Launching Bulk Payments for enterprise payout operations
excerpt: How finance teams can process thousands of payouts with stronger controls and faster reconciliation — without adding engineering complexity.
author: Altisry Product Team
content:
## Why bulk payouts break finance operations

Every finance team that has grown past a certain volume knows the problem. Payroll runs, supplier payments, commission disbursements — each one starts as a manageable task and ends as a multi-day spreadsheet exercise with too many people CCed on emails and too many manual confirmation steps in between.

The root cause is almost never the banks. It is the tooling gap between the payment initiation layer and the treasury systems that sit around it. A single batch of 3,000 supplier payments should be a fifteen-minute job. In most stacks, it is a two-day process.

We built Bulk Payments to close that gap without requiring a full replatform.

## What Bulk Payments actually does

At its core, Bulk Payments is a single API endpoint that accepts a structured payment batch — up to 10,000 recipients per batch — and handles everything downstream: routing, bank submission, retry logic, settlement confirmation, and reconciliation export.

But the real design work was in the control layer around that core:

**Role-based approval queues.** Finance teams typically have preparers and approvers. A batch prepared by an analyst should not execute without sign-off from a treasury manager. Bulk Payments enforces configurable multi-level approval before any money moves, with a complete audit trail of who approved what and when.

**Per-payment status tracking.** Not all payments in a batch will succeed on the first attempt. The execution timeline shows the real-time status of every line item — pending, submitted, settled, failed, retrying — so operators know exactly what is happening without needing to reconcile against bank statements.

**Adaptive retry policies.** Temporary bank-side failures are a fact of life in high-volume payment operations. Rather than failing an entire batch because three payments hit a network timeout, Bulk Payments queues failed items for automatic retry within configurable windows, with escalation to manual review for persistent failures.

**Reconciliation-ready exports.** Every completed batch produces a structured export — CSV, JSON, or direct webhook — mapped to treasury system fields. The goal is zero manual reconciliation work for a successful batch run.

## How integration works

For engineering teams, integration is a single REST endpoint. The request body is a batch object containing the payment array and configuration parameters. Authentication uses your existing Altisry API key. There are no separate SDKs to install and no additional OAuth flows to implement.

```json
{
  "batch_id": "payroll-2026-05",
  "payments": [
    { "recipient_iban": "GB29NWBK60161331926819", "amount": 325000, "currency": "GBP", "reference": "MAY-SALARY" }
  ],
  "approval_required": true,
  "retry_policy": "standard"
}
```

The response gives you a batch execution ID that you use to poll status or subscribe to webhooks for real-time updates.

## What comes next

The current release handles single-currency batches per submission. We are shipping multi-currency batch support in Q3, which will let treasury teams consolidate EUR, GBP, and USD payroll runs into a single API call with automatic currency routing.

Advanced scheduling windows — submit a batch now, execute at a specific settlement date — are in the pipeline for Q4, alongside configurable liquidity rules that can pause batch execution if account balances fall below a defined threshold.

For teams processing more than 50,000 payments per month, reach out to our enterprise team to discuss volume pricing and dedicated support arrangements.

title: Designing resilient webhook pipelines for payment events
excerpt: Best practices for idempotency, retry strategies, and event ordering that hold up under real production traffic — not just in demos.
author: Platform Engineering
content:
## The problem with webhook tutorials

Most webhook integration guides show you how to receive an event and write it to a database. That works in a demo. It breaks in production — usually on the day you matter most to your customers.

The failure modes are predictable. Your handler processes the same event twice because your provider retried after a 30-second timeout. Your database shows a payment as both "pending" and "settled" because two workers raced on the same event. Your reconciliation job runs at midnight and finds gaps because events arrived out of order and your system treated the gap as missing data.

These are not edge cases. They are the normal operating conditions of any payment webhook pipeline at non-trivial volume. Here is how we think about building pipelines that handle them correctly.

## Idempotency is not optional

The first rule is that every event handler must be safe to call multiple times with the same input. This is not a nice-to-have — it is the most important property a payment system can have.

The implementation has two parts. First, every incoming event needs a stable deduplication key. For Altisry webhooks, this is the `event_id` field on every payload. Store this key in a processed-events table before you touch any application state.

```sql
INSERT INTO processed_events (event_id, processed_at)
VALUES ($1, NOW())
ON CONFLICT (event_id) DO NOTHING
RETURNING event_id;
```

If the insert returns no rows, the event has already been processed — return 200 immediately without executing handler logic. This single pattern eliminates the duplicate-processing class of bugs entirely.

Second, your state transitions must be deterministic. A handler that receives a `payment.settled` event should transition a payment from `pending` or `processing` to `settled` — but should not fail or corrupt state if the payment is already `settled`. Model transitions as explicit state machine operations, not arbitrary updates.

## Retry design and backoff

Your webhook provider will retry failed deliveries. Altisry's delivery system retries with exponential backoff over a 72-hour window. The question is not whether retries will happen — they will — but whether your system handles them correctly.

Two things matter here. First, make sure your endpoint returns 200 fast. If your handler does expensive downstream work synchronously — sending emails, updating analytics, reconciling ledgers — you will hit timeout windows and generate spurious retries. The correct pattern is to acknowledge receipt immediately and push work to a queue:

```typescript
app.post('/webhooks/altisry', async (req, res) => {
  res.sendStatus(200) // acknowledge first
  await queue.push({ type: 'process_altisry_event', payload: req.body })
})
```

Second, implement dead-letter handling. After N retries from your queue processor, move failed events to a dead-letter queue and alert your operations team. Never silently discard payment events — they represent real money and real customer expectations.

## Event ordering and causal consistency

Distributed webhook delivery does not guarantee event ordering. A `payment.updated` event can arrive before the `payment.created` event it logically depends on if network partitions affect different delivery paths differently.

The wrong approach is to assume ordering and fail loudly when it breaks. The right approach is to design handlers that reconcile based on event version and resource state, not arrival order.

Each Altisry payment event carries a `version` field — a monotonically increasing integer on the payment resource. Before applying a state transition, compare the incoming event version against the version you have stored:

- If incoming version > stored version: apply the transition, update stored version
- If incoming version <= stored version: the event is stale — discard it
- If the resource does not exist yet: store the event in a pending queue keyed by resource ID, check again after processing subsequent events

This last case — handling events for resources you haven't seen a creation event for yet — is the trickiest. A bounded pending queue with a short expiry window (5–10 minutes) handles the vast majority of out-of-order deliveries without requiring complex distributed coordination.

## Observability and operations

A webhook pipeline with no visibility is a liability. At minimum you want:

- **Event receipt latency**: time from event creation to your handler receiving it
- **Processing success rate**: what percentage of received events complete successfully
- **Dead-letter queue depth**: how many events are waiting for manual review
- **Duplicate rate**: what percentage of received events are deduplicated (spikes here indicate delivery issues on the provider side)

If you are running on Altisry's platform, our developer dashboard shows delivery attempts, response codes, and retry history per event, which covers the provider side of these metrics. You need to instrument the consumer side yourself.

The payoff from getting this right is significant: payment pipelines that survive real traffic, clear audit trails when something does go wrong, and on-call engineers who sleep through the night.

title: Where open banking is heading in 2026 and beyond
excerpt: A practical view of adoption trends in lending, treasury, and account-to-account payments — and what they mean for teams building financial products today.
author: Strategy Team
content:
## From experiment to infrastructure

Four years ago, open banking was a feature you added to a fintech pitch deck. Today it is infrastructure that banks, lenders, and enterprise software companies depend on for core product functionality. That shift has changed what the interesting questions are.

The early conversation was about consent and connection coverage — can you connect enough banks, can you get users through the consent flow, will regulators actually enforce access mandates. Those questions are mostly settled in Europe. Coverage is deep, consent flows have improved significantly, and PSD2 enforcement has created a functional floor for access.

The conversation has moved to quality, composability, and cost structure.

## Lending: where open banking earned its credibility

The lending sector made the most convincing case for open banking's value, and it did it quietly. The story is not about new fintech lenders — it is about the risk teams at established consumer banks and credit bureaus who started blending open banking data into existing models and found that it worked better than expected.

The key insight is that open banking data captures cash flow signals that credit bureau data cannot: income volatility, recurring obligations, spending behaviour under financial stress. For prime borrowers, the incremental lift is modest. For near-prime and thin-file applicants — a historically underserved and commercially interesting segment — the lift is substantial.

Credit bureaus like CRIF and Experian are now treating open banking data as a first-class input, not an experimental overlay. That is a significant validation of the data quality argument and a strong signal that open banking has found durable product-market fit in credit.

**What this means for builders:** If you are in lending and not using open banking data, you are leaving accuracy improvements on the table. The integration cost is low relative to the model improvement, especially for segments where traditional data is sparse.

## Treasury: the sleeper use case

Enterprise treasury has been a slow adopter, but it is now accelerating in a way that is underappreciated by most open banking commentary.

The problem treasury teams have is visibility. A corporate with 200 bank accounts across 12 countries has no consolidated real-time cash position without significant manual work or expensive TMS integrations. Open banking APIs solve this cleanly: read-only access to all accounts, sub-minute refresh, unified data format across banks.

The adoption pattern we see is that treasury teams start with a visibility use case — consolidated cash dashboard, real-time FX exposure — and quickly find adjacent use cases: automated cash pooling, predictive liquidity modelling, anomaly detection on account activity.

The commercial dynamic here is different from consumer use cases. A corporate treasurer who saves 3 hours per week on reconciliation is extremely sticky. Enterprise open banking implementations, once deployed, have very low churn.

**What this means for builders:** The treasury use case rewards deep integration and dedicated customer success more than it rewards broad coverage. Teams serving enterprise clients should invest in the workflow layer — reporting, alerting, reconciliation exports — more than in adding incremental bank coverage.

## Account-to-account payments: the long game paying off

Account-to-account payments via open banking have had a complicated first few years. The technology works. The user experience, historically, did not.

The gap was in the authentication UX. Bank-redirected payment flows felt clunky next to card checkout. Conversion rates were disappointing and merchants were right to be skeptical.

Two things have changed this. First, embedded bank authentication — where the consent and authentication happen in a modal or native WebView rather than a full browser redirect — has materially improved the UX. Second, instant payment infrastructure (SEPA Instant, UK Faster Payments) means that settlement is genuinely fast, which changes the merchant risk calculus.

The merchants who have committed to pay-by-bank have mostly done so for cost reasons — a 0.1–0.3% per-transaction fee versus 1.5–2.5% for cards is compelling at volume — but they are finding that conversion is now competitive with cards for returning customers who have set up bank payment once.

**What this means for builders:** The conversion gap is closing but not closed. The largest UX improvement available is in the returning-customer flow — if you can avoid full bank re-authentication for customers who have consented before, you get card-like conversion. This is where product investment is paying off most in 2026.

## The infrastructure layer is consolidating

One of the clearer trends is that the open banking infrastructure layer — connectivity, normalisation, compliance — is consolidating around a smaller number of providers. The long tail of regional aggregators built on screen-scraping or fragile single-bank integrations is struggling as regulation tightens and bank APIs improve.

The providers that are growing are those with genuine API coverage, high refresh reliability, and the compliance infrastructure to serve regulated clients in multiple jurisdictions. This is a capital-intensive position to maintain, which is why consolidation makes sense.

For teams building on top of open banking infrastructure, the practical implication is that partner selection is a long-term decision. Reliability, coverage depth, and the provider's ability to stay current with regulatory change matter more than headline pricing.

## The next phase

The concepts that will matter most over the next 24 months are not new: they are the same data quality, reliability, and composability themes that have driven adoption in lending and treasury. The difference is that more sectors are now ready to act on them.

Insurance, payroll, wealth management, and B2B software platforms each have open banking use cases that are commercially viable today but haven't yet seen broad adoption. The teams that move into those sectors with production-quality integrations in the next 12 months will have a meaningful first-mover advantage before the conversation about those sectors becomes crowded.
